channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: New Deadline Looms for U.S. and Iran as Truce Wavers
item.description: Fractures were already emerging in the limited cease-fire. Vice President JD Vance will lead a U.S. delegation in talks this weekend.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance in the Oval Office with President Trump last month. Mr. Vance is to lead a U.S. delegation to Islamabad for a meeting on Saturday with Iranian officials.

item.title: After Cease-Fire, Iranians Are Left to Pick Up the Pieces
item.description: Hours after a tenuous pause to a nearly six-week war, Iranians expressed relief and trepidation about the future. Some fear the government will crack down on its domestic critics.
item.dc:creator: Sanam Mahoozi, Leily Nikounazar, Yeganeh Torbati and Shirin Hakim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times

item.title: Trump Finds Himself With Fragile Iran Cease-Fire After the Scramble to Make a Deal
item.description: After careening from one diplomatic extreme to another, President Trump finds himself with a fragile deal that is already showing signs of fraying.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager, Katie Rogers and Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s efforts to circumvent the reality on the ground in the Middle East and move into a peace process have been hampered by an adversary that continues to hold leverage.

item.title: Gilgo Beach Killer’s ‘Unusual’ Deal Could Help Solve Other Murders
item.description: Rex Heuermann agreed to talk to behavioral analysts at the F.B.I. when he pleaded guilty. The unit has interviewed Ted Bundy, John Wayne Gacy and Richard Speck.
item.dc:creator: Ed Shanahan
item.media:credit: Eduardo Munoz Alvarez/Associated Press
item.media:description: “They’re going to hopefully gain insight into the things that created him, that drove him, what causes this?” the Suffolk County district attorney, Raymond A. Tierney, said.

item.title: In 20 Minutes, Gilgo Beach Killer Admits to Murdering 8 Women
item.description: As he pleaded guilty on Wednesday, Rex Heuermann gave the same one-word answer each time he was asked how he killed one of his victims.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy
item.media:credit: Pool photo by James Carbone
item.media:description: Rex Heuermann, on left in dark suit and tie, confessed to eight murders at Suffolk County court on Wednesday.

item.title: White House Secures Foreign Steel for Trump’s Ballroom Project
item.description: ArcelorMittal, a European steel maker, is donating tens of millions of dollars of foreign steel for President Trump’s new ballroom.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: The construction site for the new White House ballroom last month.

item.title: Bondi Won’t Appear on Capitol Hill for Scheduled Epstein Deposition
item.description: Pam Bondi had already been working to avoid testifying before she was fired as attorney general. The House Oversight Committee said she would not honor her subpoena because she was no longer in the post.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pam Bondi’s stumbles in handling the Epstein investigation played a pivotal role in President Trump’s decision to fire her.

item.title: Federal Court Denies Anthropic’s Motion to Lift ‘Supply Chain Risk’ Label
item.description: The ruling was a setback for the artificial intelligence start-up in its battle with the Defense Department over the use of A.I. in warfare.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth at the Pentagon on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump Administration Investigating L.A. Schools’ Gender Disclosure Policies
item.description: The investigation into the nation’s second-largest school district was prompted by a lawsuit from parents who say the policies contributed to their child’s death.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: In January, the Supreme Court heard arguments concerning transgender athletes. Groups involved in the parents’ rights movement have helped shape the Trump administration’s priorities, including the legal and institutional protections for transgender students.

item.title: U.S. Fertility Rates Drop to Another Record Low
item.description: The fertility rate has been falling since 2007, in large part because of a plunge among teenagers.
item.dc:creator: Sabrina Tavernise
item.media:credit: Markus Scholz/picture alliance, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The fertility rate has been falling since 2007.

item.title: An Audacious $724 Million Building Reinvents LACMA
item.description: Two decades in the making, the David Geffen Galleries will offer an unconventional approach to art history and cement the director Michael Govan’s legacy.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin and Damien Maloney
item.category: Los Angeles County Museum of Art
item.media:credit: Damien Maloney for The New York Times

item.title: No, Britain Is Not Having a Christian Revival
item.description: A study said church attendance had soared among British young people, a trend reversal that excited religious conservatives around the world. Turns out it wasn’t true.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Jackson
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Churches like the St. Nicholas in Pluckley were once the beating heart of every British village, but have declined in number.

item.title: On Truth Social, Trump Supporters Fume About Iran War
item.description: A growing chorus of disaffected Trump supporters is sounding off in the replies to his posts on the social media platform he founded.
item.dc:creator: Stuart A. Thompson and Steven Lee Myers
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Blue Collar Work Has Plateaued, Narrowing Options for Young Workers
item.description: Skilled electricians, plumbers and factory workers are in demand, but job openings have dropped.
item.dc:creator: Talmon Joseph Smith
item.media:credit: Amanda Lucier for The New York Times
item.media:description: Business for Electric Avenue has picked up in recent months.

item.title: ‘Ketamine Queen’ Sentenced to 15 Years in Matthew Perry’s Overdose Death
item.description: The sentence for Jasveen Sangha, who prosecutors say was known to customers as the Ketamine Queen, is the stiffest yet for those charged in the “Friends” star’s death.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan and Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Jordan Strauss/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The medical examiner’s office attributed the actor Matthew Perry’s death to the “acute effects” of ketamine after he was found unresponsive in a hot tub at his Los Angeles home on Oct. 28, 2023.

item.title: Hasan Piker Can Stream, but Can He Stump?
item.description: The progressive and popular Twitch streamer, who is controversial with some Democrats, tests his political influence on the 2026 campaign trail.
item.dc:creator: Nathan Taylor Pemberton
item.media:credit: Nic Antaya for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dr. Abdul El-Sayed took a selfie with Hasan Piker and the crowd at a rally in Michigan on Tuesday.

item.title: Clean Energy Team Wins Salt River Project Election in Arizona
item.description: Proponents of renewable power will control the Phoenix area utility’s policymaking for the first time after they won an unusually contentious race that drew attention from national groups.
item.dc:creator: Reis Thebault
item.media:credit: Vanessa Abbitt/The Republic, via Imagn Images
item.media:description: The Salt River Project Agua Fria Generating Station in Glendale, Ariz., powers much of the Phoenix valley.

item.title: No Charges for Wisconsin Mayor Who Removed Ballot Drop Box in 2024
item.description: A special prosecutor said a review of the incident found there was not sufficient evidence to charge Doug Diny, the mayor of Wausau.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Doug Diny, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Mayor Doug Diny removing a ballot box in Wausau, Wis., in a 2024 photograph he provided.

item.title: Who Is Funding the 2026 Midterm Elections? A Lot of the Money Is Untraceable.
item.description: A lot of the money flowing into the political system is ultimately untraceable.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: The explosion in “gray money” is one of the defining trends in campaign finance.

item.title: Mamdani Acknowledges ‘Troubling’ Job Losses in 100 Days’ Interview
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani is focused on economic justice. Some budget watchdogs and business leaders want him to concentrate on economic development.
item.dc:creator: Sally Goldenberg and Dana Rubinstein
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani acknowledged concern over a recent labor report showing the city lost 19,500 private-sector jobs last year.

item.title: Jim Whittaker, First American to Reach Everest’s Summit, Dies at 97
item.description: As an executive with the outdoor-supply retailer REI and an experienced climber, he conquered Mount Everest in 1963, when fewer than 10 people were known to have done so.
item.dc:creator: John Branch
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: Jim Whittaker in 1975, before his unsuccessful attempt to summit K2, the world’s second-highest mountain. More than a decade earlier, he had successfully topped Mount Everest, the first American to do so.

item.title: Viktor Orban’s Fate Is a Warning Not to Get Too Close to Trump
item.description: The Hungarian leader’s 16-year rule could be coming to a close.
item.dc:creator: David Broder
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: NASA Flew by the Moon, but Behind the Scenes, Its Science Is a Chaotic Mess
item.description: Without science, the stunning images of Earth from space are only pretty pictures.
item.dc:creator: Kate Marvel

item.title: Stephen Colbert Skewers Trump’s ‘Double-Sided’ Cease-Fire
item.description: “The Late Show” host asked if being double-sided wasn’t a prerequisite: “I believe there’s a word for a single-sided cease-fire and it’s ‘murder.’”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: Hawaii Doctor Convicted of Attempted Manslaughter in Attack on Wife
item.description: Gerhardt Konig, 47, an anesthesiologist from Maui, attacked his wife, Arielle Konig, on a hiking trail near Honolulu, prosecutors said. He claimed it was self-defense.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Mengshin Lin
item.media:description: Gerhardt Konig on Tuesday during closing arguments in his attempted-murder trial in Honolulu.

item.title: Parts of Hawaii Face a Foot or More of Rain From Yet Another Storm
item.description: Last month was one of the wettest Marches on record for the state, leading to widespread flooding and evacuations. More rain is hitting this week.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff
item.media:credit: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times
item.media:description: Storms last month caused flooding in Waialua on Oahu and in other parts of Hawaii.

item.title: Martin Gugino, Activist Shoved by Buffalo Police at 2020 Protest, Dies
item.description: Mr. Gugino, 81, had filed a lawsuit in 2021 against the city of Buffalo and members of its police force after officers fractured his skull at a Black Lives Matter protest.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler

item.title: Half of Gen Z Uses AI, but Their Feelings Are Souring, Study Shows
item.description: A new study from Gallup found that young adults have grown less hopeful and more angry about artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Callie Holtermann
item.media:credit: Andria Lo for The New York Times
